# GeckoKit

GeckoKit is an open-source developer toolkit for creating custom Firefox-based browsers. It is not a browser, Firefox fork, or Mozilla-affiliated project. It downloads a pinned Firefox release and orchestrates Mozilla's official `mach` build system.

[Customization guide](docs/customizing.md): practical recipes for banners, custom views, toolbar buttons, new-tab replacement, CSS, prefs, branding, Firefox source overrides, and patches. Every generated project gets its own copy at `docs/customizing.md`.

## Requirements

- Linux, macOS, or Windows; Ubuntu remains the reference platform
- Node.js 22+
- Git
- About 40 GiB free for a full build, or 10 GiB for artifact mode

On macOS, install current Xcode command-line tools and Homebrew. On Windows, install MozillaBuild and Visual Studio Build Tools; GeckoKit invokes Mozilla's checked-in `mach.ps1`. Platform signing/notarization remains a release-owner responsibility.

## Create, build, run, package

```bash
npx geckokit create nim
cd nim
npm install
npx geckokit setup
npx geckokit build
npx geckokit verify
npx geckokit run
```

`nim` is only an example project name. The command accepts any safe name: `npx geckokit create <name>`.

The create wizard asks for JavaScript or TypeScript, then offers `Firefox default`, `Privacy focused`, `Minimal browser`, and `Custom` feature profiles. TypeScript browser modules use `.mts` and are transpiled to Firefox-ready `.mjs` during apply.

Artifact mode is the default recommendation for UI, CSS, JavaScript, and branding work. Run one normal artifact build first, then use the generated fast script for packaged frontend-only changes:

```bash
npm run build:fast
```

This delegates to Mozilla's `mach build faster`. Gecko, native code, WebIDL, build-system changes, WebDriver removal, and release packages require full mode. Set `build.mode` to `full`, rerun `geckokit setup`, then run `geckokit package`.

After the first build, `npm run dev` launches Firefox and watches the project. Profile CSS restarts without rebuilding; HTML, JavaScript, prefs, branding, and frontend source use `mach build faster`; config, patches, and native/build-system files use a full build. Saves are debounced and builds never overlap.

`setup` resolves the configured stable or ESR release from Mozilla product details, rejects unsupported versions, shallow-clones the matching release tag from Mozilla's official GitHub repository, records its exact revision in `geckokit.lock.json`, generates a version-compatible `mozconfig`, runs `mach bootstrap`, then applies project customizations.

`build`, `run`, and `package` always regenerate `mozconfig`, validate and apply customizations, then delegate to `mach`. `package` runs `mach build` followed by `mach package`; release packaging requires full build mode. Packages are written below:

```text
.geckokit/firefox/obj-geckokit-<binary-name>/dist/
```

Packages are unsigned. Public releases still need trademark/licensing review, replacement branding assets, signing, distribution, and updates.

## Custom browser code and UI

Use the generated project inputs:

```text
browser.config.json   typed browser/build settings
features              version-checked privacy/UI/build selections in browser.config.json
src/browser/           privileged startup module and browser/content styles
src/firefox/           files copied over matching Firefox source paths
prefs/                 native Firefox default preferences
patches/              ordered Git patches
branding/             project branding overrides
```

This follows Zen Browser's useful code-organization pattern: product code under `src/`, preferences kept separately, and upstream Firefox paths mirrored when needed. `src/firefox/` and patches can change Firefox frontend or platform code; GeckoKit supplies the reproducible source/build pipeline.

When branding is enabled, GeckoKit copies Firefox's complete version-matched `browser/branding/unofficial` tree, then applies `branding/` overrides. Replace inherited names and every platform asset before distribution.

GeckoKit owns the small AutoConfig bootstrap. Project code stays in `src/browser/startup.sys.mjs`, which runs after the first browser window is ready. The starter welcome UI is a stable native notification bar there, not a tab-bound doorhanger.

## Commands

```text
geckokit create    scaffold a project (interactive in a terminal; flags work in CI)
geckokit setup     download, bootstrap, configure, and apply
geckokit doctor    check tools, disk, source, patches, branding, and mozconfig
geckokit verify    launch an isolated profile and verify project startup
geckokit sync      restore locked source if missing, otherwise reapply changes
geckokit apply     apply overlays, branding, UI integration, and patches
geckokit export    save a .geckokit/firefox source edit into patches/
geckokit import    apply project files and patches to Firefox source
geckokit dev       watch, rebuild, and restart the development browser
geckokit build     run mach build
geckokit build faster  run Mozilla's frontend-only fast build path
geckokit run       run mach run with an isolated development profile
geckokit package   run mach build and mach package
geckokit clean     run mach clobber
geckokit mach      pass a command directly to mach
```

Git patches are checked as one ordered stack with a temporary index before any patch is applied. Already-applied patch prefixes are detected, so repeated builds are idempotent.

GeckoKit currently has compatibility records for Firefox major versions 140–153. A newly released major is refused until its Mozilla configure/bootstrap behavior is verified and GeckoKit's compatibility table is updated.
